discussion_posts.name: c4tdog
discussion_posts.content:
try to process `==` first. Then process `!=`
Some tests:
["c==c","f!=a","f==b","b==c"]
["e==d","e==a","f!=d","b!=c","a==b"]

discussion_posts.name: Arcturus_22
discussion_posts.content: This is my 500th question on Leetcode and I was able to solve it on my own. \nNot boasting, just reflecting on my progress.

discussion_posts.name: legiatuan1998
discussion_posts.content: A really good question, this is how medium should be, not too challenging, difficulty is at the right level to not make us feel overwhelm.

discussion_posts.name: etanila
discussion_posts.content: I have to learn that not every problem must be solved in one pass. The idea of two passes came to my mind after thinking for at least 15 min.

discussion_posts.name: sumit_verma_77
discussion_posts.content: First, place all `==` relationships into disjoint sets. Then, process the `!= `relationships, for `!=` ensure that the ultimate parents of the characters involved are not the same. If they are the same, it would contradict the `==` relationships.

discussion_posts.name: delta_mohit
discussion_posts.content:
Visualize the problem like this :-
Keep all the equal integers in a single line and other in different line.
Eg :- a==b, b==c, c!=d, e==a, p==q, q==r
hence, a==b==c==e, p==q==r and c!=d

Then try to think. Can't we say the whole equal section is an single component? We need to club all equal variables together.
